article_title: Fallout: New Vegas dev says Bethesda made the studio sit through 'a whole powerpoint about all the things Obsidian did wrong'
core_thesis: Chris Avellone, a writer on Fallout: New Vegas, recounts that Bethesda presented a PowerPoint to Obsidian detailing all the things Obsidian did wrong, using review scores against them to justify not continuing the partnership, despite Obsidian having delivered a successful product that kept the Fallout franchise relevant.
detailed_summary: In an interview with YouTube channel TKs-Mantis, Chris Avellone discussed the development of Fallout: New Vegas and its DLC, Dead Money. He revealed that Bethesda made Obsidian sit through a PowerPoint presentation that criticized Obsidian's work, which he described as demoralizing. Avellone also stated that Bethesda used review scores of the DLCs as a reason not to continue working with Obsidian, even though he believes Bethesda had already decided not to pursue further collaboration. The article notes that Avellone left Obsidian on bad terms and faced later controversy, so his comments should be taken with caution, but they still shed light on the strained relationship between the two studios. Avellone expressed that Obsidian thought they had made a good product that kept Fallout in the public consciousness, but Bethesda seemed unhappy despite reaping the rewards.

entities.name: Chris Avellone
entities.role: Writer on Fallout: New Vegas who recounted the PowerPoint incident

entities.name: Bethesda
entities.role: Publisher that presented the PowerPoint criticizing Obsidian

entities.name: Obsidian Entertainment
entities.role: Developer of Fallout: New Vegas that received the PowerPoint

entities.name: Fallout: New Vegas
entities.role: Game developed by Obsidian and published by Bethesda

entities.name: Dead Money
entities.role: DLC for Fallout: New Vegas that Avellone worked on and which received mixed reviews

key_data_points: Bethesda presented a PowerPoint to Obsidian detailing all the things Obsidian did wrong, which Avellone described as demoralizing and not morale-boosting.
Bethesda used review scores of the DLCs against Obsidian as a reason not to continue the partnership, even though Avellone believes Bethesda had already decided not to work with them again.
Avellone stated that Obsidian thought they had made a good product that kept Fallout in the public consciousness, but Bethesda seemed unhappy despite benefiting from the game's success.
Avellone's comments should be taken with caution as he left Obsidian on bad terms and faced later controversy (sexual misconduct allegations that were dropped in 2023).
conclusions: The article highlights the strained relationship between Bethesda and Obsidian after Fallout: New Vegas, with Bethesda using review scores as a justification for not continuing the partnership, despite the game's eventual acclaim.
Avellone's account suggests that Bethesda's criticism was perceived as unfair by Obsidian, who felt they had delivered a successful product that benefited Bethesda.

The incident underscores the importance of developer-publisher relationships and how review scores can be leveraged in business decisions, even when the product is commercially and critically successful in the long run.
personal_relevance_summary: This article provides insight into the game development industry, specifically the dynamics between developers and publishers. For a student interested in game development or project management, it illustrates how external metrics like review scores can impact business relationships. It also serves as a cautionary tale about the importance of communication and mutual respect in partnerships. While not directly related to cybersecurity or programming, it offers a real-world example of workplace politics that can inform your understanding of team dynamics and stakeholder management.
extra_advice: Consider how this dynamic applies to open-source or collaborative projects: always document agreements and maintain clear communication to avoid misunderstandings. In your own projects, whether homelab or coding, ensure that feedback is constructive and focused on improvement rather than blame.
